coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of crab-shell derived CQDs for Cd2+ detection and antibacterial applications
coredata.dc:creator: Elango, Duraisamy
Saranya Packialakshmi, Jeyakumar
Manikandan, Velu
Jayanthi, Palaniyappan
coredata.dc:description: The green synthesis of scalable high-quality fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) from natural biomass always remains an attraction. CQDs are promising materials for sensing the cadmium ions, as a class of toxic heavy metal ions, and have attracted much attention due to their chronic toxic effects on humans. The CQDs from crab shell waste were synthesized by the one-step hydrothermal method as a pioneering study and evaluated for Cd2+ ions detection and antibacterial activity. The synthesized CQDs exhibited bright green fluorescence under UV light irradiation (330 nm) and showed exorbitant quenching of Cd2+ ions in aqueous media by selective detection at the concentration range of 50–250 µM. They also exhibited antibacterial activity against two water-borne pathogens, E. coli and K. pneumonia indicating the practical applicability of CQDs in Cd2+ ions detection and antibiosis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon materials
Luminescence
Particles, nanosize
Cd2+ ion detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Metal ion detection and antibacterial activity

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) HR-TEM image, (b) SAED pattern, (c) XRD patttern, (d) FTIR spectra, (e) UV-vis absorbtion spectra, and (f) Fluorescence spectra of CQDs.
(a) Metal ion detection, (b) different metal ions, (c) Cd2+ concentrations from 50 µM to 250 µM, Zone of inhibition of CQDs towards (d) E.Coli and (d) K. pneumoniae, and (f) antibiosis mechanism.
Comparison for the CQDs synthesized from different carbon sources, synthesis routes and its application.

row.entry: Cd2+ ion detection and antibacterial activity

head.ce:title: Synthesis of crab-shell derived CQDs for Cd2+ detection and antibacterial applications

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Equal contributor as the first author.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The green synthesis of scalable high-quality fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) from natural biomass always remains an attraction. CQDs are promising materials for sensing the cadmium ions, as a class of toxic heavy metal ions, and have attracted much attention due to their chronic toxic effects on humans. The CQDs from crab shell waste were synthesized by the one-step hydrothermal method as a pioneering study and evaluated for Cd2+ ions detection and antibacterial activity. The synthesized CQDs exhibited bright green fluorescence under UV light irradiation (330 nm) and showed exorbitant quenching of Cd2+ ions in aqueous media by selective detection at the concentration range of 50–250 µM. They also exhibited antibacterial activity against two water-borne pathogens, E. coli and K. pneumonia indicating the practical applicability of CQDs in Cd2+ ions detection and antibiosis.

ce:section.ce:para: The carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are an emerging and fascinating class of carbon nanomaterials that are highly efficient in sensing of metal ions [1]. CQDs are smart and attractive nanomaterials in scientific and industrial interest receiving escalated attention in widespread applications such as sensing, medical diagnosis, photocatalysis, optoelectrical devices, and drug delivery [2]. Due to their unique properties like excellent biocompatibility, water-solubility, high photostability, and non-toxicity, attentive efforts were carried out for the synthesis of CQDs. In recent days several natural biomaterials were widely utilized for the synthesis of CQDs (Table 1 ) because it is economically feasible and environment-friendly [3–9]. The abundant availability of carbon in nature is the prime reason for increased attention divulged for the green synthesis of CQDs. In the present work, crab shells discarded as waste was used to synthesize CQDs [2]. Crab shells comprise a major raw material for the commercial production of chitin, which are excellent chelating agents utilized in metal ions detection and antibacterial agents [7]. We believe the unique composition of crab shell and their novel properties makes them multifunctional fluorescent CQDs.

The cadmium ions are frequently coexisting in numerous environmental samples with potential threats to aquatic life and humans through the food chain [8]. Cadmium ions are causing oxidative stress, genotoxicity and inhibiting DNA repair. Therefore, developing selective and sensitive analytical methods for detection of Cd2+ ions is crucial and challenged [2]. In this study, we investigated the detection of Cd2+ ions using CQDs and assessed its antibiosis potential. These, low-cost, environment-friendly CQDs based on crab shell waste offer prodigious promise in metal ion detection and antibacterial applications.

Crab shell waste were collected from marketing units. All the chemicals and reagents of analytical grades were used without further purification and deionized water was used throughout this study. The fluorescent CQDs were synthesized from crab shell powder through a simple one-step hydrothermal technique. In a typical experiment, the raw crab shells were washed with tap water, dried, and ground at room temperature. About 5 % of crab shell powder was dissolved in 100 ml of DI water and stirred for 1 h. Then, the mixture of solutions was transferred to Teflon lined stainless steel autoclave and placed in a hot air oven at 180 °C for 12 h. The resultant crude product was filtered and centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 15 min. Obtained CQDs were stored at 4 °C. The CQD solution was then powdered by vacuum freeze-drying used for further characterization. The structure and surface morphology of the prepared CQDs were analyzed by HR-TEM and X-ray diffraction (XRD). Optical properties of CQDs were determined on UV-vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu-UV-1800) and fluorescent spectrophotometer (PL, Jasco, Japan). The functional groups of fluorescent CQDs were examined using FTIR Perkin- Elmer instrument (USA). The prepared fluorescent-based CQDs were explored for the detection of cadmium ions (Cd2+) and antibacterial application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Metal ion detection and antibacterial activity
ce:section.ce:para: The methodology of metal ion detection and antibacterial activity were given in the supporting information.

Fig. 1 (a) represents the shape and size of the CQDs. The HR-TEM images showed spherical morphology of the particles about 10 nm in size. The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern (Fig. 1b) suggests deprived crystallinity of the prepared CQDs [5]. The crystalline nature of the synthesized CQDs was investigated by XRD and the results are illustrated in Fig. 1(c). XRD patterns showed a wide diffraction peak at around 24 °C owing to their small size and partial graphitization. In addition, wide peaks were perceived in the XRD pattern, confirming the crystallinity and amorphous nature of the CQDs [10]. FTIR spectra of CQDs is represented in Fig. 1(d). The FTIR results showed a broad peak at 3424 cm−1 and 2963 cm−1 which can be attributed to the existence of OH and methylene or methyl (CH) functional groups due to the presence of the aliphatic hydrocarbons and chitin in crab shells [11]. Besides, the presence of strongest peaks in the range of 1115–1000 cm−1 corresponded to COC and CO stretching respectively. The adequate availability of the COC and CH functional groups on the surface of the CQDs makes it highly hydrophilic in nature [12]. Optical properties are a crucial factor to decide the fluorescent properties of the CQDs. The UV-vis absorption spectra (Fig. 1(e)) of the crab shell CQDs in aqueous solution revealed an intense peak at UV region (∼265 nm), which corresponds to the π → π* transition of the CO band and π → π* transition of the conjugated CC band respectively. These results were spectacularly similar to earlier reported studies [13]. The photoluminescence (PL) of the CQDs was excitation wavelength-dependent, and the highest PL emission peak at 420 nm was observed after excitation at 330 nm (Fig. 1(f)). Besides, the fluorescence emission spectra show a green shift for the increase of the excitation wavelength ranging from 280 to 420 nm, which is intensely seen in the stabilized fluorescence emission spectra. Therefore, spectral properties may involve the quantum confinement, formation of aromatic structures, surface traps and might be the recombination of the excitons [9]. The stable fluorescence emission and tunable properties of CQDs are preferred to ion sensing and antibacterial applications.

To determine the selective detection of metal ions by the CQDs, different metal ions are used such as Cu2+, Fe2+, Hg2+, Ag2+, Co2+, Zn2+, and Cd2+ as illustrated in Fig. 2 (a). By examining the graph, we found that Cd2+ ions showed efficient detection with maximum (F0-F/F0) Fig. 2(b). This graph assumes the linear relationship between the relative intensity of CQDs with various metal ions. Owing to the strong interaction between CQDs and Cd2+ ions, the fluorescence intensity of CQDs got decreased. The PL intensity of CQDs decreases with the increase in the concentration of Cd2+ ions Fig. 2(c). The ability of the CQDs to detect Cd2+ was determined by their ability to fluorescence quenching property of CQDs experimented with different concentrations of Cd2+ ions (50–250 µM). Additionally, Cd2+ ions gain an electron from the ligand and reduces itself to Cd alone. It can be observed that a PL quenching phenomenon occurs upon adding cadmium solution. The emission wavelength also tends to be broader due to decreasing energy in the nanocarbon core. Fluorescence quenching of CQDs is a common mechanism, which has been revealed in previous studies for the specific sensing of heavy metal ions such as Cu2+, Fe2+, and Hg2+ ions [14]. These results indicate that the prepared CQDs have the excellent ability for selective detection of the cadmium ions in water. Further, it is a promising technique for the detection of Cd2+ ions in wastewater and industrial effluents.

The antibacterial activity (Fig. 2d and e) of synthesized CQDs with streptomycin as control was carried out against two human pathogenic bacteria. The higher concentration of CQDs (100 μg/mL) effectively inhibited the growth of E. coli (11 mm) and K. pneumonia (8 mm). These results clearly indicate that the CQDs possessed broad-spectrum of antibacterial properties [9]. The antibacterial mechanism of the CQDs may be attributed to the generation of ROS on the photocatalyst surface deactivating the bacterial cells by oxidation of their cell membranes (Fig. 2 f).

In this study, we have demonstrated a simple, and green method to prepare CQDs from crab shell wastes. The prepared crab shell CQDs had spherical morphology and exhibits an adequate degree of graphitization. Obtained CQDs emit green fluorescence under UV light illumination and excitation-dependent PL emission. Moreover, these CQDs exhibited excellent Cd2+ ion sensing and antibacterial activity.

Duraisamy Elango: Conceptualization, Methodology. Jeyakumar Saranya Packialakshmi: Validation, Formal analysis. Velu Manikandan: Validation, Data curation. Palaniyappan Jayanthi: Conceptualization, Validation, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was funded by University Grants Commission- NFST Fellowship (No: 201920-NFST-TAM-00427), India.
